sentences.base_language: Valeria works from home three days a week.
sentences.target_language: Valeria trabaja desde casa tres días a la semana.
sentences.grammarNote:
**Trabajar desde casa** = to work from home — the natural phrase in Spanish.
Also common: **trabajo remoto** (remote work), **teletrabajo** (telework/WFH).

sentences.base_language: The best part? No commute. She starts work in her pajamas.
sentences.target_language: ¿Lo mejor? Sin trayecto. Empieza a trabajar en pijama.
sentences.grammarNote:
**Lo mejor** = the best part — **lo** + adjective creates a neuter abstract noun: **lo mejor** (the best thing), **lo más importante** (the most important thing).
**Sin trayecto** = no commute — **sin** + noun (no article needed for general negation).
**Empieza** = (she) starts — **empezar** (e→ie): **empiezo, empiezas, empieza...**
**En pijama** = in pajamas — Spanish uses the singular: **el pijama** (one garment).

sentences.base_language: She sets up at her desk with coffee and opens her laptop.
sentences.target_language: Se instala en su escritorio con un café y abre la laptop.
sentences.grammarNote:
**Se instala** = she sets up/settles in — **instalarse** is reflexive: to install/set oneself up.
**El escritorio** = desk (also: computer desktop) — from **escribir** (to write).
**Abre** = (she) opens — **abrir** is a regular -ir verb: **abro, abres, abre, abrimos, abrís, abren**.
**La laptop** — anglicism used in Latin America; in Spain: **el portátil** (the portable).

sentences.base_language: At ten, she has a video call with her whole team.
sentences.target_language: A las diez tiene una reunión virtual con todo su equipo.
sentences.grammarNote:
**Reunión virtual** (virtual meeting) and **videollamada** (video call) are both used. **Tener una reunión** = to have a meeting.
Office vocabulary: **junta** (formal meeting, especially Mexico), **llamada rápida** (quick call).

sentences.base_language: 'Good morning everyone! Can everyone see my screen?'
sentences.target_language: —¡Buenos días a todos! ¿Todos pueden ver mi pantalla?
sentences.grammarNote:
**Buenos días a todos** = good morning everyone — **a todos** (to everyone) is added for emphasis.
**¿Todos pueden ver...?** — **todos** (everyone/all) can be the subject of a third-person plural verb.
**Pueden** = they/you all can — **poder** (o→ue): **puedo, puedes, puede, podemos, podéis, pueden**.
**La pantalla** = the screen — from **paño** (cloth/screen). Also: **la pantalla del teléfono** (phone screen).

sentences.base_language: The meeting lasts an hour. By the end, Valeria is hungry.
sentences.target_language: La reunión dura una hora. Al final, Valeria tiene hambre.
sentences.grammarNote:
**Dura** = lasts — **durar** is a regular -ar verb: **la película dura dos horas** (the movie lasts two hours).
**Al final** = by the end / in the end — **al** = a + el.
**Tiene hambre** = is hungry — **tener hambre** is the Spanish expression for hunger: **tengo hambre** (I'm hungry), **tiene sed** (she's thirsty), **tienen frío** (they're cold). Use **tener**, not **estar**.

sentences.base_language: She goes to the kitchen — which is the dangerous part of working from home.
sentences.target_language: Va a la cocina, que es la parte peligrosa de trabajar desde casa.
sentences.grammarNote:
**Va a la cocina** = she goes to the kitchen — **ir a** + place: **voy al trabajo** (I go to work), **va a la cocina** (she goes to the kitchen).
**Que es** — the relative pronoun **que** refers back to **la cocina**: "which is the dangerous part".
**La parte peligrosa** — adjective **peligrosa** (dangerous, feminine) follows the noun and agrees with **parte** (feminine).
**Trabajar desde casa** — infinitive as a noun: "of working from home".

sentences.base_language: In the afternoon, she tries to concentrate but gets distracted by her phone.
sentences.target_language: Por la tarde, intenta concentrarse pero se distrae con el teléfono.
sentences.grammarNote:
**Distraerse** (to get distracted) and **concentrarse** (to focus) are both reflexive. Many mental state verbs in Spanish are reflexive:
aburrirse (to get bored), preocuparse (to worry), emocionarse (to get excited)

sentences.base_language: 'Focus, Valeria,' she tells herself. 'Just one more hour.'
sentences.grammarNote:
**Concéntrate** = focus/concentrate — informal (tú) affirmative command of **concentrarse** (reflexive).
Affirmative reflexive commands: attach the pronoun to the end: **cállate** (be quiet), **levántate** (get up), **concéntrate** (focus).
**Se dice** = she tells herself — **decirse** (reflexive): talking to oneself.
**Solo una hora más** = just one more hour — **solo/solamente** (only/just), **más** (more) after the noun.

sentences.base_language: At six, she closes her laptop. The workday is over.
sentences.target_language: A las seis cierra la laptop. La jornada laboral terminó.
sentences.grammarNote:
**Cierra** = (she) closes — **cerrar** is a stem-changing verb (e→ie): **cierro, cierras, cierra, cerramos, cerráis, cierran**.
**La jornada laboral** = the workday — **jornada** (working day/shift) + **laboral** (adjective: relating to work/labor).
**Terminó** = ended/finished — preterite of **terminar** (regular -ar): the workday is now over (completed event).

sentences.base_language: She misses her coworkers a little, but honestly? She loves working from home.
sentences.target_language: Extraña un poco a sus compañeros, pero ¿la verdad? Le encanta trabajar desde casa.
sentences.grammarNote:
**Le encanta + infinitive** = she loves to do something. **Encantar** works exactly like gustar — the thing loved is the grammatical subject.
Le encanta trabajar (she loves working), me encantan las películas (I love movies). A stronger version of gustar.
